title: Measurement And Profiling
use_for: defining runtime counters and profile slices
isolating dominant cost before redesigning architecture
connecting architectural phases to measurable runtime evidence
deciding what to instrument before optimizing
do_not_use_for: micro-benchmark theater detached from runtime behavior
feature ownership with no performance concern
UI composition as a primary owner
generic logging unrelated to cost attribution
rules: measure the dominant cost before prescribing low-level changes
instrument lifecycle phases and owned work regions explicitly
prefer counters tied to real architectural boundaries
do not confuse broad telemetry with actionable profiling
procedure: Name the suspected hot phase, path, or workload.
Define the counters and timings needed to attribute cost.
Capture worst-case, average, and spike behavior under representative load.
Classify whether the dominant issue is scheduling, memory, locality, or recompute.
Escalate to the owning optimization skill with evidence.

primary_when: the request is specifically about profiling strategy, measurement setup, or cost attribution before optimization
the request is about building evidence for where runtime cost actually comes from
must_not_own_when: the bottleneck class is already known and the task is now architectural redesign in a more specific performance skill
the request is about generic logging rather than runtime cost diagnosis

solves: how to make performance diagnosis evidence-backed instead of folklore-driven
how to tie runtime metrics to real architectural boundaries
how to classify the bottleneck before choosing a redesign path
does_not_solve: the final optimization design by itself
feature-domain semantics outside the bottleneck diagnosis

core_principles: A good profile explains where cost belongs, not just that cost exists.
Instrumentation should align with lifecycle phases, queues, buffers, and owned work regions.
Worst-case spikes and backlog behavior matter as much as average timing.
constraints: Instrumentation overhead must stay low enough not to distort the target path materially.
Counters should remain tied to explicit runtime ownership rather than vague subsystem labels.
Representative load matters more than synthetic numbers detached from the real workload.
decision_rules: Use measurement-profiling as the owner when the main problem is uncertainty about what really dominates cost.
If the issue is a spike-sensitive phase, capture worst-case latency and backlog metrics before optimizing throughput.
If cost attribution cannot separate scheduling, memory, locality, and recompute, add instrumentation before changing architecture.
If the bottleneck class is already clear, hand off to the more specific performance skill instead of staying in diagnosis.

preferred_patterns: queue depth and backlog metrics where work is staged
allocation and retained-size counters tied to owned buffers
hot-path instrumentation that reflects real work boundaries
avoid_patterns: one giant performance number with no attribution
micro-benchmarks used as a substitute for runtime profiling
instrumentation labels that do not map to architecture
average-only dashboards hiding spikes and tails

recipes.name: Build a diagnosis-first profiling pass
recipes.when: Use when the runtime is slow or unstable but the dominant bottleneck class is still unclear.
steps: Mark lifecycle phases and candidate hot regions.
Add timers, counters, and backlog metrics at explicit ownership boundaries.
Run representative workloads and capture worst-case as well as average behavior.
Classify the dominant cost before selecting a redesign path.

pitfalls: Profiling everything broadly without making the numbers actionable.
Relying on averages while the real issue is tail latency or spikes.
Jumping into memory, cache, or scheduler changes before cost attribution is clear.
review_checklist: Do the metrics map to real architectural boundaries?
Can the profile distinguish spikes from averages?
Is the bottleneck class actually identifiable from the instrumentation?
Is instrumentation overhead kept proportional to the diagnosis need?

references.title: Local Reference: Measurement And Profiling
references.interpretation: Use this first for evidence-driven bottleneck diagnosis, phase-level metrics, and profiling scope decisions.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for performance measurement strategy.

references.title: Performance Domain Foundations
references.interpretation: Use this to keep profiling aligned with the domain's cost model, budgeting doctrine, and optimization boundaries.
references.why: Use to keep diagnosis grounded in the larger performance doctrine.
